text.title: Ollama Production Deployment — Core Rules
text.summary: Five testable rules that gate any application of the ollama-deployment methodology. Sources: Ollama documentation — server / serve flags; NVIDIA CUDA + container-toolkit docs; Nginx reverse proxy guidance for streaming LLM APIs.

rule.statement: Container deployments MUST pass GPU access (--gpus all for Docker run, or deploy.resources.reservations in Compose); without it inference silently falls back to CPU with no warning in logs.
rule.rationale: Ollama does not emit a warning when GPU is unavailable — it simply uses CPU at 5-20x slower inference speed, which can go undetected in production.

rule.statement: Bind Ollama to 127.0.0.1:11434 (not 0.0.0.0) in production; expose only through a reverse proxy with authentication and rate limiting.
rule.rationale: Ollama has no built-in authentication; binding to all interfaces exposes model access to the network without credentials.

rule.statement: Set proxy_buffering off in nginx for all Ollama location blocks; buffering blocks streaming responses and causes long perceived latency.

rationale.source: Nginx reverse proxy guidance for streaming LLM APIs

rule.rationale: Without buffering disabled, nginx accumulates the full response before sending, negating the UX benefit of streaming generation.

rule.statement: Mount ollama_data as a named volume for model persistence; do not rely on container-local storage — models are multi-gigabyte and re-pulling on every container restart is unacceptable.
rule.rationale: Container restart without volume mount deletes all pulled models; re-pulling a 70B model takes 30+ minutes.

rule.statement: Validate that the produced `config` matches the schema in `content/02-output-contract.xml` before handing off (rule r5).
rule.rationale: Mechanical validation prevents downstream consumers from receiving malformed artefacts; the validator script enforces this at PR time.
